sources.title: UK Habitat Classification v2.01
sources.specific_claim: c1b definition and hierarchy — ryegrass/clover ley as UKHab level 4 cropland code

ecology.description: Ryegrass/clover ley (c1b) is an intensively managed, sown grassland dominated by perennial ryegrass (Lolium perenne) with white clover (Trifolium repens), established by reseeding and managed for high forage production. It is a temporary or rotational habitat, typically lasting 3–5 years before quality declines and reseeding is warranted. It has low ecological value as a habitat but very high agricultural production value. The white clover component fixes nitrogen, reducing synthetic N inputs, and improves nutritional quality. Sward quality, expressed as the proportion of target species and the nutritional value of the herbage, declines progressively from year 1 onwards as weed grasses (annual meadow grass, Yorkshire fog, bent grasses) encroach and as ryegrass thins with age.
ecology.ecological_value_notes: Minimal ecological value. Monoculture or near-monoculture of improved ryegrass. Low plant diversity, low invertebrate value, no specialist wildlife habitat. Ecological value score 1 (lowest) is appropriate. This is not a conservation target — it is a production tool.
condition_indicators_good: Perennial ryegrass dominant (>70% cover), dense and leafy
White clover well established and visible throughout (target 25–35% DM contribution in grazing season)
No significant weed grass species present — annual meadow grass, Yorkshire fog, bent grasses <10% cover combined
Dense basal ground cover — minimal bare ground or open patches
Sward height managed consistently — not rank or stemmy
Clean entry to grazing at 2,500–3,000 kg DM/ha, grazed down to 1,500 kg DM/ha
condition_indicators_poor: Annual meadow grass (Poa annua) >20% cover — low yield, low quality, unpalatable
Yorkshire fog (Holcus lanatus) present — low palatability, rapidly reduces sward quality
Ryegrass thinning — open patches, declining basal density
Clover declining or absent — loss of N fixation and quality benefit
Rank or stemmy growth — D-value falling, wasted utilisation
Poaching damage, compaction, or waterlogging reducing ground cover
ecosystem_functions: Very high DM production — primary function. Supports high livestock stocking rates.
Nitrogen fixation via white clover — can supply 150–280 kg N/ha/yr, reducing synthetic N requirement
Soil organic matter contribution — ryegrass root system adds OM between reseeds
Carbon: net carbon neutral to slight sink under good management; reseeding events are disturbance with short-term carbon cost

sources.title: Clover species for grassland reseeding
sources.specific_claim: White clover fixes 150 kg N/ha typically, up to 280 kg N/ha; optimum clover content 30% DM in grazing sward; can increase yields up to 15%

sources.title: Understanding grass growth for beef rotational grazing
sources.specific_claim: PRG has three live leaves per tiller; four-leaf stage triggers leaf death and wastage; grazing at 2.5–3 leaf stage optimal; rest sward above 1,250–1,500 kg DM/ha

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Prime / Established
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Sward in years 1–3 post-reseed. PRG + WC >80% cover by target species. Dense basal cover. No significant weed grass ingress. White clover well established at 25–35% DM contribution. Maximum production potential. Nutritional quality high throughout the season.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Good / Minor decline
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Years 2–4. Target species 60–80%. Minor annual meadow grass ingress beginning. Clover still present and functional. Ryegrass thinning slightly at base. Production still good; nutritional quality maintained with management. Reseed horizon visible.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Moderate / Declining
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Years 3–5. Target species 40–60%. Notable weed grass encroachment — AMG, Yorkshire fog becoming significant. Clover declining or patchy. Ryegrass base thinning. Production and quality falling. Reseed warranted within 1–2 seasons.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Poor / Weed-dominated
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Target species <40%. Weed grasses dominating. Significant open patches, bare ground, or compaction damage. Clover largely absent. Production low; nutritional quality poor. Urgent reseed warranted — sward delivering little over a permanent pasture baseline and without the ecological value.

condition_grade_descriptors.label: Transitional / Reclassify
condition_grade_descriptors.description: Target species <20%. Weed grasses, bent, or rushes dominant. No longer functioning as a productive ley. Reclassify as g4 (modified permanent grassland) or g3c (other neutral grassland) depending on composition. Reseed essential.

grade_field_indicators: Target species cover (%) — walk representative transects assessing PRG + WC combined. Use 1m² quadrats at 5 points minimum per field.
Weed grass cover (%) — identify annual meadow grass (flat, pale, horizontal tillers), Yorkshire fog (soft grey-green, hairy), bent grasses (fine, wiry). Any >10% combined is Grade 3 signal.
Clover cover and health — white clover stolons present and actively growing; estimate % DM contribution visually or by hand-plucking sample.
Basal density — part sward at ground level; >80% green basal cover = Grade 1; open patches and dead material = Grade 3–4.
Sward height and uniformity — check for rank areas, stemmy patches, or uneven grazing pressure.
Weed broadleaves — docks, buttercup, chickweed presence and % cover. Flag for herbicide or management action.
Evidence of compaction or poaching — check especially at gateways, water points, and fence lines.

sources.title: Check swards for forage potential
sources.specific_claim: Sward health check methodology including target vs weed species % assessment; NIAB year-1 ley 16 t DM/ha declining to 12 t by year 3; weed grass identification criteria
sources.notes: L5 — extension/trade press citing NIAB data. Core assessment methodology sound and consistent with AHDB guidance.

sources.title: Three key steps to selecting varieties
sources.specific_claim: Sward composition assessment; disease can cause losses >1 t DM/ha over 3-year period; seasonal growth distribution by heading date

forage_yield_by_grade.notes: Commercial grazing utilisation target for UK/Ireland is 10 t DM/ha (AHDB). NIAB figures: year-1 ley capable of 16 t DM/ha under cutting/trial conditions; year-3 ley ~12 t. Commercial grazing realises 60–70% of trial potential due to trampling, utilisation losses, and less intensive management. Grade 1 range anchored to commercial utilisation, not trial maxima. Well-managed PRG/WC ley should achieve 10–12 t utilised. Top RGCL varieties exceed 11 t DM/ha in trials (RGCL 2025/26).
forage_yield_by_grade.yield_context_note: CRITICAL DISTINCTION: RGCL trial yields (16–18 t DM/ha) are under newly sown plots with high N inputs and optimal cutting regimes. Commercial farm utilisation is consistently lower. Always present commercial utilisation figure to clients, not trial maximum.

forage_yield_by_grade.notes: Estimated from NIAB year-3 decline trajectory and AHDB utilisation data. Weed grass ingress and clover decline at this grade reduce both yield and quality. Estimated reduction of 20–30% from Grade 1.

Estimated. Weed grasses at 40–60% of sward produce significantly less DM than PRG and are less palatable, reducing effective utilisation further. Yorkshire fog in particular reduces animal intake. NIAB noted weed grasses range from 7–28% of PRG production potential — applied as degradation factor.

Estimated. Weed-dominated sward with <40% target species. Production approaching g4 (modified permanent grassland) values. At this grade, reseed economics are compelling. Value in this range estimated from weed grass productivity data in NIAB sward assessment guidance.

forage_yield_literature.notes: Commercial utilisation range 8,000–12,000 kg DM/ha for well-managed Grade 1 ley. Trial/optimal conditions (RGCL) 16,000–18,000 kg DM/ha — not representative of farm-scale performance. Literature consistently distinguishes trial potential from commercial utilisation. The 10 t DM/ha target for commercial grazing systems is the primary reference for client-facing use.

sources.title: Recommended Grass and Clover Lists for England and Wales 2025/26
sources.specific_claim: Top PRG varieties capable of >11 t DM/ha in trials; RGCL trial conditions 16–18 t DM/ha under cutting with high N; yields expressed as % of variety mean

sources.title: Grass leys for the arable rotation
sources.specific_claim: RGCL trial yields 16–18 t DM/ha under cutting with high N and newly sown plots. Commercial grazing target: utilise 10 t DM/ha. Utilisation varies 50–80%.

sources.title: Check swards for forage potential (NIAB figures cited)
sources.specific_claim: NIAB: year-1 ley 16 t DM/ha, declining to 12 t by year 3. Weed grasses produce 7–28% of PRG potential.

nutritional_quality_by_grade.me_notes: Fresh leafy PRG in active growth. AHDB GrassCheckGB data shows spring peak 12.1–12.2 MJ/kg DM, autumn recovery ~12 MJ/kg DM. Summer can dip to 11.5 MJ/kg DM or below if sward allowed to stem. Well-managed rotational grazing maintains ME at upper end of range. Average metabolizable energy density of PRG reported at 11.5 MJ/kg DM (ScienceDirect).
nutritional_quality_by_grade.cp_notes: Leafy ryegrass contains 16–25% CP in DM (NIAB/ADAS). AHDB seasonal data: CP peaks ~24.3% in March, declines to ~21% by July, recovers to 24.4% by November. White clover is higher CP than ryegrass — mixed sward with 30% clover contribution boosts overall CP. 80% of CP in fresh grass is true protein.
nutritional_quality_by_grade.d_value_notes: Top RGCL varieties in 75–80 D-value range (Germinal/NIAB). One percentage point of D-value = 0.16 MJ/kg DM ME. D-value highest in fresh leafy growth; declines after ear emergence.

sources.title: Forage for Knowledge end of year review 2023
sources.specific_claim: Seasonal ME data from farm monitoring: spring peak 12.1–12.2 MJ/kg DM; summer below 12 MJ; autumn recovery to 12 MJ. CP seasonal range: March 24.3%, July 21%, November 24.4%.

sources.title: The Nutritional Value of Grass
sources.specific_claim: Leafy ryegrass 16–25% CP in DM; NIAB: 1 D-value point = 0.16 MJ/kg ME = 0.26 litres milk/cow/day; optimum NDF 30–40% DM; top varieties 75–80 D-value range

nutritional_quality_by_grade.notes: Minor weed grass ingress begins to dilute nutritional quality. Annual meadow grass and Yorkshire fog lower D-value than PRG. Clover still contributing but at lower cover. Quality still good with careful management.

Estimated. Weed grasses at 40–60% of sward pull down nutritional quality significantly. Yorkshire fog particularly low D-value and unpalatable — animals avoid it, further reducing effective intake. Clover largely absent — N fixation lost, protein contribution falling.

Estimated. Weed-dominated sward approaching permanent pasture nutritional values but without the ecological value of true permanent pasture. Animal performance will be poor; supplementation likely required. Reseed urgently warranted.

optimal_grazing_animals.notes: PRG/WC ley is the primary habitat for dairy systems. ME and CP values at Grade 1 are ideal for milk production.
Highly suited — including suckler herds and finishing cattle. Rotational grazing advised.
Well suited for finishing lambs and ewes. White clover at high cover — monitor bloat risk.
Suitable for outdoor pig systems on well-drained ground with careful management to avoid poaching.

timing_windows.preferred_season: Spring through autumn — full growing season
timing_windows.grazing_rotation_notes: Rotational grazing at 18–25 day intervals in peak season maintains sward at 2.5–3 leaf stage for PRG — optimal quality and production. Adjust to 30–35 days autumn/spring shoulder. Enter sward at 2,500–3,000 kg DM/ha, exit at 1,500 kg DM/ha minimum.
timing_windows.avoid: Grazing below 1,500 kg DM/ha damages sward — delays recovery and allows weed grass establishment. Avoid heavy cattle grazing on wet or poached ground — compaction and structural damage accelerate grade decline.

management.intensity_guidance: High production sward — designed for intensive utilisation under rotational management. Overgrazing threshold: below 4 cm / 1,500 kg DM/ha. Undergrazing threshold: above 3,000 kg DM/ha — sward becomes stemmy, D-value falls rapidly after ear emergence. Monitor clover content — target 25–35% DM contribution over grazing season. If clover falling, review nitrogen management (mineral N suppresses clover) and grazing pressure.
management.monitoring_protocol: Spring: full sward assessment — target vs weed species %, clover cover estimate. Monthly through season: plate meter readings at paddock entry and exit to track DM availability and utilisation. Annual: photograph fixed points. Flag weed grass encroachment >15% for action — herbicide or reseed decision. Track ley age — reseed economics trigger at Grade 3 (years 3–5 typically).

fields_partially_populated: forage_yield_by_grade — Grade 1 good confidence; Grades 2–4 medium/low — estimated from degradation trajectory
nutritional_quality_by_grade — Grade 1 high confidence; Grades 2–4 estimated
fields_pending_research: Grades 2–4 yield and nutritional data — requires dedicated SRUC/AFBI trial data on degrading ley swards specifically
Mineral data (Ca, P, Mg, K, Na, Cu, Co, Se) for nutritional_quality — present in AFBI/ADAS datasets but not retrieved in this session
Anti-nutrients: ryegrass staggers (endophyte) documented — needs specific source for toolbox
Bloat risk quantification for white clover content thresholds
data_completeness.notes: Grade 1 yield and nutritional quality for c1b is the best-sourced production sward record in the toolbox. RGCL (NIAB/AFBI/SRUC collaborative, L2), AHDB GrassCheckGB (L2), and ADAS/NIAB nutritional value guide (L5) give coherent, consistent data. The critical distinction between trial yield (16–18 t DM/ha) and commercial utilisation (10 t DM/ha target) is explicitly sourced and must be maintained in client-facing outputs. Grade 2–4 yield and nutritional decline are estimated from trajectory data — sourcing AFBI or SRUC ley degradation trial data would upgrade these to good/high confidence. Mineral data gap should be filled from AFBI nutritional tables in a follow-up session.
